questions.stem: A public health nurse is performing TB contact tracing after identifying an active pulmonary TB case in an elementary school teacher. The school has 420 students and 35 staff. Which group should be prioritized for PPD/IGRA testing and evaluated first?

options.text: All community residents within a 1-mile radius of the school
Close contacts — students in the infected teacher's classroom and staff sharing the teachers' lounge (highest-risk exposure contacts)
Only the principal and vice-principal as the administrative contacts
All 420 students simultaneously to ensure no child is missed

questions.rationale: TB contact investigation follows a concentric circle approach. Priority 1 — close contacts (highest risk): household members, individuals with prolonged face-to-face exposure in an enclosed space. For a school teacher: students in the infected teacher's classroom (daily prolonged contact), colleagues sharing enclosed spaces (teachers' lounge, planning periods). Priority 2 — casual contacts: students in the same school but different classrooms. Priority 3 — community contacts. All close contacts receive TST/IGRA regardless of symptom status. Positive contacts → chest X-ray → LTBI vs active TB determination → treatment. Mass testing of all 420 students before stratifying risk wastes resources and creates unnecessary anxiety.

distractorRationales.a: This option makes All community residents within a 1-mile radius of the school the focus, without accounting for Priority 3 — community contacts. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so Close contacts — students in the infected teacher's classroom and staff sharing the teachers' lounge (highest-risk better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The clinical error in Only the principal and vice-principal as the administrative contacts is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by Priority 1 — close contacts (highest risk): household members, individuals with prolonged face-to-face exposure in an enclosed space. Close contacts — students in the infected teacher's classroom and staff sharing the teachers' lounge (highest-risk is safer because it addresses circulation or perfusion before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: Selecting All 420 students simultaneously to ensure no child is missed would be unsafe, while the decisive cue is Priority 2 — casual contacts: students in the same school but different classrooms. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay circulation and perfusion support and leave circulation or perfusion unresolved.

questions.takeaway: TB contact tracing: concentric circle approach — prioritize closest contacts first (classroom students, shared-space colleagues); cascade outward only if high conversion rate found in inner circle.

questions.stem: A nurse has four patients to assess. Which patient should the nurse see FIRST, applying Maslow's hierarchy of needs?

options.text: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure
A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%
A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home
A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10

questions.rationale: Maslow's hierarchy places physiological needs at the foundation. Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. A patient with RR 8 and SpO2 88% is in respiratory distress — an immediate life-threatening concern that takes absolute priority. Anxiety (A) is a safety/psychological need. Discharge teaching (C) is a self-actualization/cognitive need. Pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure.

distractorRationales.a: Selecting A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure would be unsafe, while the decisive cue is Pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home, rather than acting on Pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The safer answer is A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88% because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10, despite the clinical warning in Pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: Maslow hierarchy on NCLEX: airway/breathing/circulation (physiological) always come before safety, love/belonging, esteem, and self-actualization.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an admission assessment on a 79-year-old patient transferred from a nursing facility. Which combination of factors identifies the HIGHEST fall risk?

options.text: Age >65 and female gender
History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg on standing)
Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29
Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion

questions.rationale: The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls in the elderly; (2) Polypharmacy with antihypertensives — alpha-blockers, diuretics, and calcium channel blockers commonly cause orthostatic hypotension and syncope; (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Both the Morse Fall Scale and STRATIFY risk tool weight prior falls most heavily. Key interventions: bed in lowest position, non-skid footwear, call light within reach, scheduled toileting (most falls occur during nighttime toileting), orthostatic vital signs before ambulation, and urgent medication review. Age and gender alone are poor predictors without functional factors.

distractorRationales.a: Selecting Age >65 and female gender would be unsafe, while the decisive cue is (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of medication toxicity and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: This answer choice treats Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29 as the key issue, instead of responding to (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of medication toxicity and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion, while the decisive cue is (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so Choosing it can delay medication-safety intervention and leave medication toxicity unresolved.

questions.takeaway: Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Prior fall is the strongest single predictor. Target modifiable risk factors aggressively.

questions.stem: An RN is caring for four patients and needs to delegate tasks. Which task is appropriate to assign to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP/CNA)?

options.text: Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea
Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube
Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery
Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care

questions.rationale: UAPs can perform tasks that are routine, repetitive, and do not require nursing judgment or assessment. Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the RN has assessed the patient's stability. Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Administering tube feedings (B) — in many states, enteral feeding via established G-tube can be delegated to trained UAPs, but the skill level required is greater than ambulation; this varies by state — ambulation is universally appropriate for CNA delegation. Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment.

distractorRationales.a: This option overweights Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea, although the nurse should be guided by UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.b: The reasoning trap in Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, without accounting for UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching. Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: Selecting Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care would be unsafe, although the nurse should be guided by Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the. Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so The priority is Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching, and medication administration remain with the RN.

questions.stem: A patient delivers vaginally and 30 minutes later has an estimated blood loss of 750 mL with a soft, boggy uterus displaced to the right. BP is 94/58, HR 118. Which sequence of interventions is correct?

options.text: IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider
Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement
Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion
Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction. (2) Empty the bladder — a full bladder displaces the uterus and prevents contraction (uterus displaced to the right = full bladder). (3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic. (4) Notify provider if no response. Bladder emptying is critical and often overlooked — the displaced, boggy uterus signals bladder distension. Epinephrine (D) and hysterectomy are not indicated at this stage.

distractorRationales.a: IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider would not address the priority, when the bedside priority is driven by Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so It is less defensible because Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion the focus, when the bedside priority is driven by Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent, instead of responding to (4) Notify provider if no response. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so The nurse should prioritize Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement; otherwise bleeding or anticoagulation injury remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: Boggy uterus displaced to the right after delivery = full bladder; empty bladder first, then fundal massage + oxytocin.

questions.stem: A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth, and swelling. Vital signs are stable. Which action is the NURSE'S PRIORITY?

options.text: Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses
Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result
Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound
Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring

questions.rationale: Unilateral calf pain, warmth, and swelling in a post-operative patient are classic signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT). The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can be ordered promptly. Homan's sign (B) is unreliable and has been removed from clinical guidelines. Warmth and elevation (A) delay diagnosis. Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate.

distractorRationales.a: This option overweights Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses, although the nurse should be guided by Unilateral calf pain, warmth. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay circulation and perfusion support and leave circulation or perfusion unresolved.
distractorRationales.b: The reasoning trap in Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, even though the higher-risk finding is Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate. Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring, even though the higher-risk finding is The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Post-op calf pain + swelling = suspect DVT; notify provider and anticipate duplex ultrasound — Homan's sign is unreliable.

questions.stem: A client with depression says: 'I don't see the point in trying anymore. Nothing I do matters.' Which nurse response is most therapeutic?

options.text: 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.'
'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'
'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.'
'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'

questions.rationale: Option B is an open-ended exploration that invites the client to elaborate, demonstrating active listening without judgment. It also screens for suicidal ideation — 'nothing I do matters' and 'no point in trying' may signal passive suicidal thinking. Option A uses false reassurance ('so much to live for') and assumes understanding without exploring further. Option C minimizes the client's experience ('everyone feels that way'). Option D is non-therapeutic confrontation that dismisses the client's feelings.

distractorRationales.a: This choice focuses on 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.', rather than acting on therapeutic communication safety. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is 'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.' because delaying bedside safety action can worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: This answer choice treats 'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.' as the key issue, rather than acting on therapeutic communication safety. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay bedside safety action and leave clinical safety risk unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: 'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.' is not the best answer, without accounting for therapeutic communication safety. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so 'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.' is safer because it addresses clinical safety risk before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Therapeutic communication: use open-ended statements to explore meaning, especially when statements may signal suicidal ideation.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with pneumonia who is receiving 4L/min via nasal cannula. The SpO2 is 91%. The nurse repositions the patient and administers the scheduled nebulizer treatment. Which finding would indicate the interventions were effective?

options.text: Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%
The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television
Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer
The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min

questions.rationale: Effective respiratory interventions produce measurable objective improvements in gas exchange: SpO2 rising to ≥ 94-95% and respiratory rate normalizing toward < 20/min indicate improved ventilation and oxygenation. Decreased anxiety may reflect improved oxygenation but is subjective and non-specific. Increased breath sounds after a nebulizer can indicate mobilization of secretions (which is initially normal), not necessarily a therapeutic response. Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions.

distractorRationales.b: This choice focuses on The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television, despite the clinical warning in The SpO2 is 91%. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: The clinical error in Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, when the bedside priority is driven by work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min, without accounting for Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.

questions.takeaway: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

visualRationale.caption: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.
visualRationale.conclusion: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing vital signs in the pediatric unit. Which set of vital signs is within normal limits for a healthy 3-year-old toddler?
questions.rationale: Normal vital signs for a 3-year-old toddler: HR 80–130 bpm, RR 22–37 breaths/min, BP approximately 90–100/55–65 mmHg. HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 are all within expected ranges. Choice A has a bradycardic HR and adult-level BP. Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress, and RR 40 is elevated. Choice D has significant bradycardia. Key developmental milestone: vital signs normalize toward adult ranges with age — neonates have the highest HR and RR, lowest BP.

distractorRationales.a: This option overweights HR 68, RR 14, BP 120/80, rather than acting on toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: This option overweights HR 145, RR 40, BP 80/48, while the decisive cue is toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: HR 55, RR 18, BP 110/72 would not address the priority, when the bedside priority is driven by Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

visualRationale.caption: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.
visualRationale.conclusion: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy at 8:00 AM. Per current ASA NPO guidelines, what are the correct restrictions for solid food and clear liquids?

options.text: Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid
Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery
Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, clear juice, black coffee) allowed until 2 hours before
No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk

questions.rationale: Current ASA NPO guidelines (2017, updated 2023): Solid foods, fried/fatty meals: NPO ≥8 hours. Light meals (toast, clear liquids): NPO ≥6 hours. Non-clear liquids including milk and formula: NPO ≥6 hours. Clear liquids (water, apple juice without pulp, clear carbonated beverages, black coffee or tea without cream, sports drinks): may be consumed up to 2 hours before elective procedures. Chewing gum and hard candy: treat as clear liquid — stop 2 hours before. These evidence-based guidelines replaced the outdated 'NPO after midnight' rule. The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance, and perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor pulls attention toward Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid, when the bedside priority is driven by High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.b: This option makes Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery the focus, without accounting for High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk, but the stem cue is High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water,, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before; clear liquids until 2 hrs before. 'NPO after midnight' is outdated and harmful. Clear liquids: water, clear juice (no pulp), black coffee/tea.
